metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion for `churn` that asserts exact summed insertions and deletions over a two-commit numstat fixture (matching the test description in the Tests section).
evidence: AC2 covers only `refactor_to_addition_ratio`; no AC names `churn` or asserts its summed insertions/deletions values
The Files section says `churn ... → git log --numstat ... → summed insertions/deletions` but no AC requires asserting those totals
The Tests section mentions 'a git log --numstat fixture over a range yields the expected churn totals' yet no AC captures this as a verifiable criterion
findings.finding: The `churn` function is specified in the Files section and Tests section but has no acceptance criterion. An executor implementing it has no formal, verifiable done-state for that function.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section (all four ACs) vs. Files section (`churn(repo_root, since, until)`)
scenarios: An executor ships a broken `churn` implementation and all four ACs still pass — the plan provides no checkpoint that catches it.
A reviewer has no AC to sign off against for `churn`, leaving its correctness unverified at acceptance time.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] `churn` over the two-commit numstat fixture returns `{insertions: N, deletions: M}` with values matching the fixture's line counts — asserted exactly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC1 to assert `near_cap_count` and `over_cap_count` against fixture modules deliberately placed within 10% of the cap and over the cap respectively.
evidence: AC1 says 'returns `max_loc` equal to the largest fixture module's line count and `count` equal to the number of `.py` modules scanned (exact-value asserts)' — `near_cap_count` and `over_cap_count` are not mentioned
The Files section defines `near_cap_count (within 10% of the 800 cap)` and `over_cap_count` as part of the return value
findings.finding: `near_cap_count` and `over_cap_count` are specified as keys in the `module_size_distribution` return dict but neither is asserted in AC1 or any other AC, leaving those fields unverified at acceptance time.
findings.location: AC1 and Files section — `module_size_distribution` return dict
scenarios: An executor returns `near_cap_count=0` always (or omits `over_cap_count`) and all ACs pass — the critical safety metric (count within N lines of the cap) is never exercised.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC1: '… and `near_cap_count` equals the count of fixture modules whose LOC is within 10% of the fixture cap, and `over_cap_count` equals the count of fixture modules exceeding the cap — all exact-value asserts.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC assertions for `near_cap_count` (fixture module within 10% of cap → near_cap_count ≥ 1) and `over_cap_count` (fixture module exceeding cap → over_cap_count ≥ 1) to close the uncovered-deliverable gap.
evidence: Files: 'module_size_distribution … → {count, near_cap_count (within 10% of the 800 cap), max_loc, over_cap_count}'
AC1 asserts only 'max_loc equal to the largest fixture module's line count and count equal to the number of .py modules scanned' — no mention of near_cap_count or over_cap_count
findings.finding: `near_cap_count` and `over_cap_count` are named return-value fields in the Files section's `module_size_distribution` spec but neither field is asserted in any acceptance criterion, leaving two described deliverables without coverage.
findings.location: Files section — `module_size_distribution` return dict `{count, near_cap_count, max_loc, over_cap_count}`; AC1
scenarios: An implementer returns near_cap_count=0 always or omits over_cap_count and all ACs still pass.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the fixture to include one module at 98% of the cap (triggers near_cap_count) and one above the cap (triggers over_cap_count), and add exact-value asserts for both fields in AC1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that asserts `churn()` returns `None` over a range with no commits and returns `0` (not `None`) over a range whose commits have no file changes, verifying the None/0 distinction explicitly.
evidence: Approach explicitly names this as a contract: 'distinguishes that "no data accrued" case from a genuine measured zero'
No AC references None-vs-0 behavior for any metric
findings.finding: The Approach's None/0 distinction (compute returns `None` for no-data vs `0` for genuine zero churn) is a described behavioral contract but has no corresponding acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Approach section — 'each git-metric compute returns None … when its range yields no commits … a real churn of 0 over a populated range returns 0, not None'
scenarios: An implementation always returns None (or always returns 0) and passes all ACs; downstream consumers of the metrics registry receive wrong Unavailable signals.
findings.suggested_fix: Add two fixture scenarios: (a) a date range before any commits → assert result is None; (b) a commit that touches only non-Python metadata with 0 insertions+deletions → assert result is 0.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `module_size_distribution` counts lines using `content.count('\n')` (matching the CI gate's `wc -l` semantics, as already established in `test_module_size_contract.py`) so the metric is consistent with enforcement.
evidence: Plan: 'parse src/rebar/**/*.py LOC (via git ls-tree/git show at the ref)'
test_module_size_contract.py line 42: `loc = path.read_text(...).count('\n')` — explicitly matches CI gate's wc -l semantics
ABSENCE: no specification of newline-count vs splitlines() vs other method in the plan
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the line-counting semantics for `module_size_distribution`, creating a risk that the metric disagrees with the CI gate. The existing gate and `test_module_size_contract.py` use `text.count('\n')` (newline counting, matching `wc -l`); `len(content.splitlines())` differs for files without a trailing newline. An unspecified method could produce `near_cap_count`/`over_cap_count` values inconsistent with what CI would enforce.
findings.location: Files section — `module_size_distribution`; Approach section
scenarios: A module with no trailing newline is counted as 799 lines by `splitlines()` but 798 by `count('\n')` — the metric reports it as 'within 10% of cap' when CI would flag it as over-cap at cap=800
Discrepancy between metric-reported `over_cap_count` and CI failure surprises operators
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'LOC is counted as `content.count("\n")` to match the CI gate's `wc -l` semantics (mirroring `test_module_size_contract.py`).' Add an AC fixture that includes a file lacking a trailing newline and asserts the expected count.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the return value of `refactor_to_addition_ratio` when the range has zero insertions (e.g. return `None` to signal 'undefined' per the registry contract, or `float('inf')`, documenting the choice) and add an AC covering a deletion-only fixture.
evidence: Plan: 'a real churn of 0 over a populated range returns 0, not None'
Plan: 'refactor_to_addition_ratio(repo_root, since, until) — git log --numstat over the range → summed insertions/deletions and the deletions:insertions ratio'
The ratio deletions/insertions is undefined when insertions=0 (e.g. a range that only deletes lines) — the plan has no specified return value for this case
findings.finding: `refactor_to_addition_ratio` has an unhandled division-by-zero when a commit range contains only deletions (insertions=0). The plan says a 'real churn of 0 returns 0, not None' but doesn't distinguish 'empty range' (return None) from 'deletion-only range' (insertions=0, ratio undefined). A caller passing a pure-refactor/deletion range would get a ZeroDivisionError.
findings.location: Files section — `churn` / `refactor_to_addition_ratio`; Approach section — None/zero semantics
scenarios: A date range covering only commits that delete code (test cleanup, dead-code removal) yields insertions=0; computing the ratio raises ZeroDivisionError
AC2 tests a 'delete-heavy' fixture but doesn't clarify whether insertions could be 0 in that fixture — if it uses at least one insertion it masks the bug
findings.suggested_fix: Return `None` when `total_insertions == 0` (the registry contract already maps `None` → `Unavailable`, which is the right signal for 'ratio undefined'); add a third fixture commit variant — a pure-deletion commit — to the test and assert the function returns `None` for that range.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact `MetricSpec` `id` strings that each new git-metric function will be registered under in `REGISTRY`, and clarify whether they replace existing seed entries or are additional entries.
evidence: registry.py REGISTRY: existing ids are 'module_size_trend', 'oversized_module_count', 'commit_cadence_trend'
Plan AC4: 'the metrics register into src/rebar/metrics/registry.py with source="git"' — no ids named
Plan Files section names functions module_size_distribution, cap_change_events, churn, refactor_to_addition_ratio — these are function names, not metric spec ids
findings.finding: The plan never specifies what MetricSpec `id` strings the new entries will carry in REGISTRY. The three existing seed entries have IDs `module_size_trend`, `oversized_module_count`, `commit_cadence_trend`. AC4 asserts the metrics 'register into registry.py' but gives no IDs to check, making the assertion unverifiable and leaving open whether the new specs replace/update existing seeds or add alongside them.
findings.location: Files section (git_metrics.py), Acceptance Criteria AC4
scenarios: Implementer picks arbitrary ids (e.g., 'churn_30d') that don't match the eventual metric command's expected surface, causing downstream naming inconsistency
Implementer inadvertently re-uses or collides with 'module_size_trend', which already exists as a structural-source seed — the REGISTRY unique-id invariant would fail

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how each multi-parameter git-metric function is adapted into the single-argument `compute` callable the `MetricSpec` / `evaluate()` contract requires (e.g., explicit lambda/partial pattern, or a context object schema that bundles repo_root + range).
evidence: Plan: 'module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref="HEAD")' and 'churn(repo_root, since, until)' are named with multi-parameter signatures
registry.py line 61: 'compute: Callable[[Any], Any]' — single-arg callable
registry.py lines 103–122: evaluate() calls spec.compute(context) with one argument
Plan never mentions functools.partial, lambda wrappers, or a context object that bundles the parameters
findings.finding: The plan's four named functions (module_size_distribution, cap_change_events, churn, refactor_to_addition_ratio) take multi-parameter signatures (e.g., repo_root, ref, since, until) but the existing registry compute contract is Callable[[context], value|None] — a single positional argument. The plan never specifies how these functions are adapted into single-argument compute= callables for MetricSpec registration.
findings.location: Files section — git_metrics.py function signatures; registry.py compute contract (MetricSpec.compute: Callable[[Any], Any])
scenarios: Implementer registers module_size_distribution directly as compute= and gets a TypeError when evaluate() calls it with a single context argument
Implementer creates per-metric lambdas but the plan gives no guidance on what the context carries, making the AC4 registration test ambiguous to write
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the Files section: each function is wrapped as `compute=lambda ctx: module_size_distribution(ctx.repo_root, ctx.ref)` (or similar), and specify what fields the context object carries — OR describe that `compute` callables are `functools.partial(fn, repo_root=..., ...)` closures baked at registration time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism for retrieving `old_limit` in `cap_change_events` (e.g., `git show <commit>^:.github/module-size-limit.txt` for each matching commit) and add an AC assertion that both `old_limit` and `new_limit` are correct numeric values in the fixture.
evidence: Plan: 'git log over .github/module-size-limit.txt in range → list of (commit, old_limit, new_limit)'
`git log --follow -- <file>` gives commit SHAs but not the before/after content; AC3 requires 'old and new limit values' to be reported
No mention of `git show <sha>^:.github/module-size-limit.txt` or `git diff <sha>^..<sha>` to retrieve the old value
findings.finding: The cap_change_events spec asserts it yields (commit, old_limit, new_limit) tuples from git log over .github/module-size-limit.txt, but the mechanism for extracting old_limit is unspecified — `git log` alone does not emit before/after file content; a per-commit `git show <parent>:file` diff is required, which is a non-trivial capability not verified in the plan.
findings.location: Files section — `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)` spec; Acceptance Criteria — AC3
scenarios: Implementor uses `git log --diff-filter=M -- .github/module-size-limit.txt` but has no spec for how old_limit is retrieved, leading to an implementation that only returns new_limit or omits old_limit entirely.
AC3 fails because the fixture only tests the new limit, not the old, revealing the gap at test time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact git invocation used to extract `old_limit` and `new_limit` in `cap_change_events` — e.g., `git log -p --format=%H -- .github/module-size-limit.txt` with diff parsing, or `git show <sha>~1:<file>` + `git show <sha>:<file>` per commit — and handle the edge case where the file was first created (no parent to diff against).
evidence: Plan text: 'git log over .github/module-size-limit.txt in range → list of (commit, old_limit, new_limit)'

`git log` with a pathspec yields commits but not file contents; extracting old_limit/new_limit requires `git show <sha>~1:.github/module-size-limit.txt` and `git show <sha>:.github/module-size-limit.txt` per commit, OR `git log -p` diff parsing — neither is named
Evidence class: Partially-verified (git log over a path is real; the content-extraction sub-surface is Unverified as described)
findings.finding: The plan specifies `cap_change_events` returns `(commit, old_limit, new_limit)` using `git log` over `.github/module-size-limit.txt`, but `git log` alone yields commit SHAs and messages — NOT the old and new file contents. Recovering `old_limit` and `new_limit` requires an additional per-commit invocation of `git show <parent>:<file>` and `git show <commit>:<file>`, or parsing `git log -p` diff output. This is a real sub-surface the plan does not name, leaving the implementation without a specified mechanism for the most load-bearing part of `cap_change_events`.
findings.location: Files section — `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)` description; Acceptance Criteria AC-3
scenarios: An implementer using only `git log -- .github/module-size-limit.txt` gets commit SHAs but must independently discover the need for `git show` or `-p` parsing to populate old_limit/new_limit.

If `git log -p` is used, the implementer must correctly parse unified diff output for a single-integer file — a non-obvious parsing task with edge cases (e.g., first commit that created the file has no parent).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `cap_change_events` description: 'Use `git log --format=%H <since>..<until> -- .github/module-size-limit.txt` to list relevant commits, then for each SHA recover old_limit via `git show <sha>~1:.github/module-size-limit.txt` (returns empty/error for the initial creation commit, treated as old_limit=None) and new_limit via `git show <sha>:.github/module-size-limit.txt`.' Also add an AC that explicitly asserts the initial-creation edge case (where old_limit is None or absent).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `context` object schema (e.g., a `GitContext(repo_root, ref, since, until)` dataclass or dict) that the registry's `evaluate(spec, context)` will pass, and specify that git-metric `compute` callables accept this single object — co-locate this in the Files or Approach section.
evidence: Plan: 'Registered into the c085 registry' (Approach section) — but registry.py `MetricSpec.compute: Callable[[Any], Any]` and `evaluate(spec, context=None)` pass a single argument.
Plan defines: `module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref='HEAD')` — 2 params; `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)` — 3 params; `churn(repo_root, since, until)` — 3 params.
registry.py line 61: `compute: Callable[[Any], Any]`; line 111: `result = spec.compute(context)` — single call site with one arg.
ABSENCE: no plan text specifies a `context` dataclass, dict schema, or lambda-wrapping pattern to bridge the gap.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that git-metric functions with multi-argument signatures (`repo_root`, `ref`, `since`, `until`) are 'registered into registry.py', but the registry's `MetricSpec.compute` contract is a single-`context` callable (`Callable[[Any], Any]`) and `evaluate` passes exactly one argument. The plan never defines what the `context` object contains or how multi-argument functions are bound into the single-argument slot, leaving the implementor with an unresolved adapter protocol.
findings.location: Files section — `module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref='HEAD')`, `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)`, `churn(repo_root, since, until)` / `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` MetricSpec.compute signature
scenarios: An implementor wraps `churn` as a bare function reference but `evaluate` calls it with only `context` — TypeError at runtime.
An implementor uses a lambda/partial but the `since`/`until` parameters are baked in at registration time, making the metric non-reusable across date ranges.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'Each git metric is registered with a `compute` that accepts a single `GitContext(repo_root, ref, since, until)` dataclass (or `dict` with those keys); the function extracts the fields it needs and ignores the rest. `ref` defaults to `"HEAD"` when absent from context.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback for git subprocess failures in the Approach section: state whether `CalledProcessError` (and similar) should propagate, be caught and re-raised with context, or be returned as `None` with a logged warning — and distinguish this from the legitimate 'no data accrued' `None`.
evidence: Plan: 'each git-metric `compute` returns `None` (never `[]`/`{}`/`0`) when its range yields no commits or the source file is absent' — covers only data-absence, not subprocess failure.
ABSENCE: no plan text specifies whether a `CalledProcessError` from `git log` should be caught and re-raised, returned as `None`, or converted to a structured error.
The registry's `evaluate` has no try/except — an uncaught exception from `compute` propagates to the caller.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `compute` returns `None` only for 'no commits in range' or 'source file absent', but does not specify the fallback when a git subprocess itself fails (non-zero exit code, not a git repo, permission error). This is a distinct failure mode from 'no data accrued' and is unaddressed.
findings.location: Approach section — 'each git-metric `compute` returns `None` (never `[]`/`{}`/`0`) when its range yields no commits or the source file is absent'
scenarios: CI runs `evaluate` in a shallow clone where `git log --numstat` fails — exception propagates instead of graceful `Unavailable`.
An implementor silently swallows all subprocess errors as `None`, making a broken git environment indistinguishable from 'no data accrued yet'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact return types for `churn` (e.g. `{'insertions': int, 'deletions': int}`) and `refactor_to_addition_ratio` (e.g. `float`, rounded to N decimal places or exact fraction) in the Files section.
evidence: 'churn ... → summed insertions/deletions and the deletions:insertions ratio' — no dict keys, no type stated
Contrast with `module_size_distribution` which explicitly lists `{count, near_cap_count, max_loc, over_cap_count}` — the same specificity is absent for the other two functions
findings.finding: The return types of `churn` and `refactor_to_addition_ratio` are unspecified — the plan says 'summed insertions/deletions' and 'deletions:insertions ratio' but does not state whether these are dicts, floats, namedtuples, or scalars. An executor must guess the structure.
findings.location: Files section — `churn` and `refactor_to_addition_ratio` descriptions
scenarios: One executor returns `churn` as a flat tuple `(ins, del)`, another as `{'insertions': N, 'deletions': M}` — both satisfy the prose but are incompatible with callers.
The AC2 numeric assertion cannot be written unambiguously without knowing the return type of `refactor_to_addition_ratio`.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, add to `churn`: '→ `{"insertions": int, "deletions": int}`' and to `refactor_to_addition_ratio`: '→ `float` (deletions / insertions; `None` if insertions == 0 or no commits in range)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile Approach and AC4 by either (a) quoting the c085 registry contract that `confidence='high'` satisfies `is_authoritative()`, or (b) replacing the parenthetical 'authoritative' with the exact attribute/flag the registry exposes and that AC4 tests.
evidence: Approach: 'source="git", confidence="high" (authoritative)'
AC4: 'is_authoritative("git") is True — asserted'
Registry contract (c085) is not quoted in the plan as mapping confidence="high" to is_authoritative()=True
findings.finding: The Approach section specifies `confidence="high"` as the registry attribute, but AC4 verifies `is_authoritative("git") is True` — these are different API surfaces and the plan never establishes that setting `confidence="high"` causes `is_authoritative()` to return True, creating a cross-section mismatch between how the property is set and how it is tested.
findings.location: Approach section — 'confidence="high" (authoritative)'; AC4 — 'is_authoritative("git") is True'
scenarios: Implementer sets confidence='high' on the metric but the registry's is_authoritative() looks at a different attribute (e.g., a separate boolean flag), so AC4 fails despite Approach compliance — or vice versa.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the `near_cap_count` proximity threshold a named parameter (e.g., `near_cap_pct=0.10`) with a documented default rather than a hardcoded literal, so consumers can override it and the value is visible at call sites.
evidence: 'near_cap_count (within 10% of the 800 cap)' is stated as a literal, not as a named constant or parameter
The cap itself is correctly single-sourced, but the proximity threshold is not
No mention of making this threshold a parameter or a named constant
findings.finding: The `near_cap_count` threshold of 10% is asserted as a fixed value in the function description with no mechanism to configure or override it. If the team decides the alerting window should be 5% or 20%, every consumer must be updated ad-hoc.
findings.location: Files section — `module_size_distribution` description: 'near_cap_count (within 10% of the 800 cap)'
scenarios: Team decides 50 LOC from cap (6.25%) is a better warning band — requires changing function internals and all callers
Different consumers may want different near-cap sensitivities
findings.suggested_fix: Change the signature to `module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref='HEAD', near_cap_pct=0.10)` and derive the threshold as `cap * near_cap_pct` internally; document the default in the docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for git subprocess calls: wrap calls in try/except, apply a reasonable timeout (e.g., 30 s), log a warning with the stderr/exception on failure, and return `None` so the c085 registry surfaces `Unavailable` rather than propagating an exception or hanging.
evidence: Plan states 'compute returns None when its range yields no commits or the source file is absent' — covers expected-empty cases only
No mention of try/except around subprocess calls, timeouts, or logging of subprocess stderr
'authoritative' and 'high confidence' framing suggests failures should be surfaced, not silently dropped
findings.finding: The plan specifies no timeout, error handling, or observability for git subprocess failures (non-zero exit, command not found, or hung process). The `None` return covers the 'no data' case but not unexpected subprocess errors, leaving the failure mode unspecified.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Everything is deterministic and read-only. Distribution scans the tree at a ref…'; Files section — `churn`, `cap_change_events`, `module_size_distribution` implementations
scenarios: git is not on PATH in a container environment — subprocess raises FileNotFoundError, propagates uncaught to the registry
git log hangs on a network-mounted repo — metrics computation blocks indefinitely with no timeout
git returns exit code 128 (not a git repo) — unspecified whether this raises, returns None, or logs a warning

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `git_metrics.py`'s git subprocess calls go through `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git` (the codebase's single shared git seam) rather than raw `subprocess.run`, per the precedent set by `git_adapter.py` and `_store/sync.py`.
evidence: `src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py` docstring: 'consolidates the dozen hand-rolled `_git()` wrappers that had drifted into a different signature/return/error contract each' — `run_git` is 'that shape once'

`src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/git_adapter.py` docstring: 'Every `git` subprocess the reconciler runs flows through this module … built ON TOP of `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git` — the one shared `subprocess.run(["git", …])` shape for the whole codebase'
The plan's Approach section describes `git log --numstat`, `git ls-tree`/`git show`, and `git log` over limit file history with no mention of `run_git`
`_churn_90d` in `workflow_ops.py:326` is an existing example of the anti-pattern (raw `subprocess.run` for git) that `run_git` was created to prevent from proliferating further
findings.finding: The plan introduces git subprocess calls in `git_metrics.py` without referencing `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git`, the codebase's single shared git subprocess seam created specifically to consolidate ad-hoc `subprocess.run(["git", ...])` calls.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py` — Approach section: 'git log --numstat', 'git ls-tree'/'git show'
scenarios: Implementor writes fresh `subprocess.run(["git", "-C", repo_root, "log", "--numstat", ...])` calls, adding more drift variants of the same shape that `run_git` was built to eliminate.
Future codebase audit finds yet another git invocation style to consolidate, increasing maintenance burden.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section, note that git invocations in `git_metrics.py` use `from rebar._store.gitutil import run_git` rather than raw `subprocess.run`, mirroring the pattern in `git_adapter.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan how `old_limit` is recovered for each commit (e.g. `git show <commit>^:.github/module-size-limit.txt`) and how the initial-commit case (no parent) is handled — e.g. `old_limit=None` — so the implementation is unambiguous.
evidence: Plan: 'git log over .github/module-size-limit.txt in range → list of (commit, old_limit, new_limit)'
git log output is commit SHAs + metadata; content recovery requires a separate git show invocation per commit
ABSENCE: no mention of how old_limit is read from the parent commit, or what happens when the introducing commit has no parent (e.g. initial repo commit)
findings.finding: `cap_change_events` says it yields `(commit, old_limit, new_limit)` but `git log` over the file only identifies WHICH commits touched it — it doesn't yield the before/after content. Recovering `old_limit` requires reading the parent blob (`git show <commit>^:.github/module-size-limit.txt`), which fails with a fatal error when the commit is the first commit to that file (no parent). The plan doesn't specify the blob-reading strategy or the initial-commit edge case.
findings.location: Files section — `cap_change_events(repo_root, since, until)`
scenarios: Repository bootstrapped with module-size-limit.txt as part of the first commit — `git show <sha>^:...` fails with 'unknown revision', causing a subprocess error or incorrect `old_limit`
A cap change range spanning the very first edit to the limit file misreports `old_limit` as None/error rather than a clean 'no previous value' signal
findings.suggested_fix: For each commit from `git log`, run `git show <commit>:.github/module-size-limit.txt` for `new_limit` and `git show <commit>^:.github/module-size-limit.txt` for `old_limit`; catch non-zero return (no parent) and set `old_limit=None` for that event.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in the plan that `git_metrics.py` will use `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git` (the codebase's canonical git subprocess wrapper) for all git invocations, not a new bespoke wrapper.
evidence: src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py docstring: 'consolidates the dozen hand-rolled _git() wrappers that had drifted into a different signature/return/error contract each'

Plan: 'git ls-tree/git show at the ref, or a tree walk' — describes raw git commands without naming the wrapper
findings.finding: The plan does not mention `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git` — the codebase's canonical, shared git subprocess wrapper — as the implementation vehicle. The module docstring of `gitutil.py` explicitly exists to 'consolidate the dozen hand-rolled _git() wrappers that had drifted into a different signature/return/error contract each'. Omitting it from the plan risks introducing a new bespoke wrapper, repeating the exact anti-pattern the utility was created to eliminate.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py`; Approach section
scenarios: Implementer writes a new `def _git(repo_root, *args): subprocess.run(...)` in git_metrics.py, recreating the drift the utility was designed to prevent
Timeout/error-handling contract diverges from established wrappers, causing inconsistent behavior under git failures
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files section: 'All git subprocess calls use `from rebar._store.gitutil import run_git` — the canonical shared wrapper — to maintain consistent error-handling and timeout contracts across the codebase.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the `git ls-tree` invocation for `module_size_distribution`: use `git ls-tree -r <ref> -- src/rebar/` (recursive, no glob) and filter `.py` files in Python, OR use the `:(glob)` magic pathspec — and note this explicitly so implementers don't pass a bare `**/*.py` glob that git silently ignores.
evidence: Plan text: 'parse src/rebar/**/*.py LOC (via git ls-tree/git show at the ref, or a tree walk)'
git ls-tree does not expand shell globs natively; `**` requires the `:(glob)` magic pathspec or recursive `-r` + post-filter
Evidence class: Partially-verified (git ls-tree -r exists and is real; the glob-pattern sub-surface is Unverified as described in the plan)
findings.finding: The plan describes scanning `src/rebar/**/*.py` via `git ls-tree` but does not confirm that the glob pattern `**/*.py` is passed correctly as a git pathspec. Passing `src/rebar/**/*.py` as a shell glob to `git ls-tree` without the `:(glob)` magic pathspec prefix causes git to treat `**` as a literal double-star and silently return no results on most git versions. The correct approach is either `git ls-tree -r <ref> -- src/rebar/` with Python-side `.py` filtering, or using `:(glob)src/rebar/**/*.py`. The plan's phrasing is ambiguous enough that an implementer could silently get an empty result set.
findings.location: Files section — `module_size_distribution(repo_root, ref)` description
scenarios: Implementer passes `src/rebar/**/*.py` directly to git ls-tree argv → git returns 0 results silently → `module_size_distribution` returns `{count: 0, ...}` → unit test with a fixture tree fails or, worse, passes with wrong values if the fixture also hits the same bug.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `module_size_distribution` description, write: 'Use `git ls-tree -r --name-only <ref> -- src/rebar/` and post-filter for lines ending in `.py` in Python (git ls-tree does not expand shell globs; pass the directory, not a glob pathspec).'
